title: The ANE Efficiency Advantage
scenario: Your team is evaluating mobile SoCs for an always-on image classification app. The Apple A17 Pro advertises 35 TOPS from its Neural Engine at a sustained power draw of approximately 5W. What is the compute efficiency in TOPS per watt, and why does this metric matter more than raw TOPS for a mobile device?

details.common_mistake: Focusing only on peak TOPS without considering power. The Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 advertises 73 TOPS but at similar power, so raw TOPS alone does not determine which chip is better for a battery-constrained workload.
details.realistic_solution: 35 TOPS / 5W = 7 TOPS/W. On a mobile device, the binding constraint is the battery, not peak compute. A chip that delivers 7 TOPS/W means each watt of battery power yields 7 trillion INT8 operations per second. This is the metric that determines how many inferences you can run before the battery dies.
details.napkin_math: A17 Pro ANE: 35 TOPS / 5W = 7 TOPS/W. At 18.5 Wh battery, running the ANE at full power drains the battery in ~3.7 hours.
options: A) 3.5 TOPS/W. Mobile chips sacrifice efficiency for portability.
B) 7 TOPS/W. The battery is the binding constraint, so efficiency per watt determines inference budget.
C) 35 TOPS/W. The 5W figure is idle power, not compute power.
D) 175 TOPS/W. Neural Engines achieve superlinear efficiency at low precision.

title: The 48MP Camera Firehose
scenario: A modern flagship phone has a 48MP camera sensor shooting 10-bit RAW at 30 fps burst mode. Before any ISP processing or compression, what is the raw data bandwidth flowing from the sensor to the SoC?

details.common_mistake: Confusing the compressed HEIF/JPEG output size (a few MB per photo) with the raw sensor bandwidth. The ISP must handle the full uncompressed firehose before any compression occurs.
details.realistic_solution: 48 million pixels x 2 bytes (10-bit packed into 16-bit) x 30 fps = 2.88 GB/s. This is why mobile SoCs need dedicated high-bandwidth MIPI CSI interfaces and why the ISP is a critical bottleneck, not the CPU or GPU.
details.napkin_math: 48MP x 2 bytes x 30 fps = 2.88 GB/s raw sensor bandwidth. This rivals the LPDDR5X memory bandwidth of the entire SoC.
options: A) ~30 MB/s. Each 48MP frame is about 1MB compressed.
B) ~288 MB/s. 48MP at 1 byte per pixel at 6 fps.
C) ~2.9 GB/s. 48MP at 2 bytes per pixel at 30 fps, uncompressed.
D) ~29 GB/s. 48MP at 20 bytes per pixel with full color depth.

title: The On-Device Fine-Tuning Data Budget
scenario: You want to fine-tune a mobile vision model on-device using 1,000 user photos preprocessed to 224x224 RGB tensors in FP32. How much memory does this training dataset occupy, and does it fit comfortably in the 8GB unified memory of an iPhone 15 Pro alongside the model?

details.common_mistake: Estimating based on JPEG file sizes (a few MB total) rather than the uncompressed tensor representation that the training loop actually requires in memory.
details.realistic_solution: 1,000 images x 224 x 224 x 3 channels x 4 bytes (FP32) = 602 MB. This is significant but fits in 8GB alongside a quantized model. However, training also needs gradients and optimizer states, which can 2-3x the memory pressure.
details.napkin_math: 1,000 x 224 x 224 x 3 x 4 bytes = 602 MB. Model (INT8, 25M params) = 25 MB. Gradients + optimizer = ~75 MB. Total ~700 MB, fits in 8 GB.
options: A) ~6 MB. JPEG images are small and efficient.
B) ~60 MB. Each tensor is about 60 KB.
C) ~600 MB. Each 224x224x3 FP32 tensor is ~600 KB, times 1,000 images.
D) ~6 GB. Deep learning datasets always require gigabytes.

title: The INT4 Quantization Payoff
scenario: Your team wants to deploy a 3B parameter language model on a phone with 8GB unified memory. The INT8 version is 3 GB. A colleague proposes INT4 quantization to free memory for the KV cache. How much memory does the INT4 model save, and what is the primary risk?

details.common_mistake: Assuming INT4 is always a free win. The 1.5 GB savings is real, but INT4 quantization can cause significant accuracy degradation on tasks requiring precise numerical reasoning, and not all layers quantize equally well.
details.realistic_solution: INT4 halves the weight memory: 3B x 0.5 bytes = 1.5 GB, saving 1.5 GB compared to INT8. This freed memory can hold a KV cache of ~1,500 tokens at FP16 for a 7B-class model. The risk is accuracy loss, especially in embedding and attention output layers.
details.napkin_math: INT8: 3B x 1 byte = 3.0 GB. INT4: 3B x 0.5 bytes = 1.5 GB. Savings = 1.5 GB. Fits in 8 GB with room for KV cache + OS.
options: A) 0.75 GB savings. INT4 is only 25% smaller than INT8.
B) 1.5 GB savings. INT4 uses half the bytes of INT8, but risks accuracy loss in sensitive layers.
C) 3.0 GB savings. INT4 eliminates half the parameters entirely.
D) No savings. INT4 still requires INT8 storage with a lookup table.

title: The Battery Inference Budget
scenario: A mobile health app runs continuous pose estimation at 30 fps, consuming 5W sustained on the ANE. The phone has an 18.5 Wh battery. The product manager wants the app to run for 8 hours. Is this feasible with inference alone, ignoring all other phone functions?

details.common_mistake: Ignoring that 5W is only the ANE power draw. The display, radios, and OS consume another 2-3W minimum, so actual battery life is much shorter than the naive calculation suggests.
details.realistic_solution: 18.5 Wh / 5W = 3.7 hours from inference alone. Adding display and OS overhead (~3W), total draw is ~8W, giving only ~2.3 hours. The 8-hour target is physically impossible without reducing inference frequency (e.g., 5 fps instead of 30 fps, cutting power to ~1W).
details.napkin_math: Inference only: 18.5 Wh / 5W = 3.7h. With system overhead: 18.5 Wh / 8W = 2.3h. At 5 fps (1/6 duty cycle): ~1W inference + 3W system = 4W, giving 18.5/4 = 4.6h. Still short of 8h.
options: A) Yes. 18.5 Wh / 5W = 3.7h of inference, plus the OS is free.
B) No. Inference alone drains the battery in 3.7h; with system overhead it is ~2.3h. Must reduce frame rate.
C) Yes. The ANE uses only milliwatts for neural inference.
D) No, but only because the phone will thermal-throttle first.

title: The Depthwise Separable Convolution Dividend
scenario: You are porting a server-side image classifier to mobile. The heaviest layer is a standard 3x3 convolution with 256 input and 256 output channels. A colleague suggests replacing it with a depthwise separable convolution. By what factor does this reduce the multiply-accumulate operations per spatial position?

details.common_mistake: Guessing a 2-3x reduction. The actual savings scale with the number of output channels and are much larger, which is why MobileNet achieved near-ResNet accuracy with 8-9x fewer FLOPs.
details.realistic_solution: Standard 3x3 conv: 256 x 256 x 9 = 589,824 MACs per position. Depthwise separable: (256 x 9) + (256 x 256) = 2,304 + 65,536 = 67,840 MACs. Ratio: 589,824 / 67,840 = 8.7x reduction.
details.napkin_math: Standard: C_in x C_out x K^2 = 256 x 256 x 9 = 590K MACs. DW-sep: C_in x K^2 + C_in x C_out = 2.3K + 65.5K = 67.8K MACs. Savings: ~8.7x.
options: A) ~2x. Depthwise separable splits one convolution into two, roughly halving the work.
B) ~4x. The savings come from removing the kernel spatial dimensions.
C) ~8.7x. The factorization eliminates the C_in x C_out x K^2 cross-channel multiply.
D) ~27x. The savings scale cubically with the kernel size.

title: The On-Device LLM Memory Wall
scenario: You want to run a 7B parameter LLM in INT4 quantization on an iPhone 15 Pro (LPDDR5X at ~51.2 GB/s bandwidth, 8 GB unified memory). During autoregressive token generation, the model must read all weights from memory for each token. What is the maximum token generation rate, and what is the binding constraint?

details.common_mistake: Assuming the ANE's 35 TOPS is the bottleneck. In autoregressive decoding, the arithmetic intensity is ~1 op/byte (each weight is used for a single token), making this heavily memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound.
details.realistic_solution: 7B params x 0.5 bytes (INT4) = 3.5 GB per token read. At 51.2 GB/s memory bandwidth, each token takes 3.5/51.2 = 68 ms, yielding ~15 tokens/second. The binding constraint is memory bandwidth, not compute. The ANE sits mostly idle.
details.napkin_math: Model size: 7B x 0.5B = 3.5 GB. Time per token: 3.5 GB / 51.2 GB/s = 68 ms. Rate: ~15 tok/s. Arithmetic intensity: ~1 op/byte (batch=1), far below ANE ridge point.
options: A) ~150 tok/s. The 35 TOPS ANE can process tokens in parallel.
B) ~15 tok/s. Each token requires reading the full 3.5 GB model, bounded by 51.2 GB/s bandwidth.
C) ~1.5 tok/s. INT4 dequantization creates a compute bottleneck.
D) ~1,500 tok/s. Unified memory eliminates the bandwidth wall.

title: The KV Cache Memory Squeeze
scenario: You are running a 7B LLM on a phone with 8 GB unified memory. The model uses 32 layers, 32 attention heads, and a head dimension of 128. At a context length of 2,048 tokens in FP16, how much memory does the KV cache consume, and does the system still fit in memory alongside the INT4 model weights?

details.common_mistake: Forgetting that the KV cache stores both keys AND values for every layer, or underestimating by only counting one layer. The KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and can easily rival the model weight memory.
details.realistic_solution: KV cache = 2 (K+V) x 32 layers x 2048 tokens x 32 heads x 128 dim x 2 bytes (FP16) = 1.07 GB. Model weights (INT4) = 3.5 GB. Total = 4.57 GB. Fits in 8 GB, but leaves only ~3.4 GB for OS, apps, and activations. At 4096 tokens, the KV cache doubles to 2.15 GB, making it very tight.
details.napkin_math: KV cache: 2 x 32 x 2048 x 32 x 128 x 2 = 1.07 GB. Model: 3.5 GB. Sum: 4.57 GB. Headroom in 8 GB: ~3.4 GB for OS + activations.
options: A) ~107 MB. The KV cache only stores the current layer's attention state.
B) ~1.07 GB. Keys and values across all 32 layers add up fast at 2K context.
C) ~4.3 GB. The KV cache is the same size as the model weights.
D) ~10.7 GB. The system cannot fit in 8 GB at any context length.

title: The Vocabulary Embedding Tax
scenario: A mobile LLM uses a 32K token vocabulary with a 4096-dimensional embedding layer stored in FP16. How large is the embedding table, and what fraction of a 3.5 GB INT4 model does it represent if the embeddings cannot be quantized below FP16 without severe quality loss?

details.common_mistake: Treating the embedding table as a trivially small lookup. For LLMs, the embedding and unembedding layers can be a significant fraction of total memory, and they are often the hardest to quantize aggressively.
details.realistic_solution: 32,000 x 4,096 x 2 bytes = 262 MB. This is 262/3500 = 7.5% of the INT4 model. If the embeddings must stay at FP16 while other layers go to INT4, the effective compression ratio of the full model is worse than advertised.
details.napkin_math: Embedding table: 32K x 4096 x 2B = 262 MB. Fraction of 3.5 GB model: 262/3500 = 7.5%. Un-quantizable overhead.
options: A) ~26 MB. Embeddings are just a small lookup table.
B) ~262 MB. 32K vocab at 4096 dims in FP16 is a quarter-gigabyte, 7.5% of the model.
C) ~1 GB. Embeddings dominate the model footprint.
D) ~2.6 MB. Embeddings compress well to INT4 like other layers.

title: The Speculative Decoding Gambit
scenario: On a mobile device, your main 7B LLM generates at 5 tokens/second (memory-bandwidth-bound). You add a 150M draft model that runs at 20 tokens/second. The draft model proposes 4 tokens at a time, and on average 3 are accepted by the verifier. What is the effective token throughput with speculative decoding?

details.common_mistake: Assuming speculative decoding simply adds the two models' speeds. The key insight is that the large model verifies all 4 draft tokens in a single forward pass (same cost as generating 1 token), so you get multiple tokens for the price of one verification.
details.realistic_solution: Draft time: 4 tokens / 20 tok/s = 0.2s. Verification: 1 forward pass of large model = 0.2s. Total: 0.4s for 4 accepted tokens (3 accepted + 1 from verification). Effective: 4/0.4 = 10 tok/s, a 2x speedup over the baseline 5 tok/s.
details.napkin_math: Draft: 4 tok / 20 tok/s = 200ms. Verify: 1/5 = 200ms. Total: 400ms for 4 tokens. Effective: 10 tok/s. Speedup: 2x over baseline 5 tok/s.
options: A) 25 tok/s. The draft and main model speeds simply add together.
B) 10 tok/s. Draft takes 200ms, verification takes 200ms, yielding 4 tokens per 400ms cycle.
C) 5 tok/s. Speculative decoding cannot improve throughput on a bandwidth-bound device.
D) 20 tok/s. The draft model does all the work and the verifier only rejects occasionally.

title: The Cellular Model Delivery Problem
scenario: Your app needs to deliver a 4 GB INT4 language model to users over cellular. The median LTE download speed is 50 Mbps. A product review flags that users on metered data plans will be unhappy. Calculate the download time and propose a deployment strategy that respects mobile network constraints.

details.common_mistake: Confusing megabits (Mb) with megabytes (MB). 50 Mbps = 6.25 MB/s, not 50 MB/s. Also, ignoring that cellular connections are bursty and real throughput is often 30-50% of advertised speeds.
details.realistic_solution: 4 GB / 6.25 MB/s = 640 seconds = 10.7 minutes at peak. Real-world with overhead: 15-20 minutes. Strategy: (1) Ship a base model in the app bundle, (2) download full model over WiFi only with user consent, (3) use delta updates for model version upgrades (~5-10% of full size), (4) stream initial layers while downloading the rest.
details.napkin_math: 50 Mbps / 8 = 6.25 MB/s. 4,000 MB / 6.25 MB/s = 640s = 10.7 min. On metered plan at $10/GB: $40 data cost. WiFi-only download with delta updates (200 MB) reduces subsequent updates to ~32s.

title: The Federated Learning Upload Bill
scenario: You are designing a federated learning system for a mobile keyboard app. Each training round involves 1,000 devices uploading a 50 MB model diff. The system runs 10 rounds per day. Calculate the total daily upload bandwidth and explain why naive federated learning is infeasible at scale without compression.

details.common_mistake: Only considering a single device's upload cost (~500 MB/day), which seems manageable. The system-level cost across all participating devices reveals the true infrastructure burden.
details.realistic_solution: 50 MB x 1,000 devices x 10 rounds = 500 GB uploaded per day. At median mobile upload speed of 10 Mbps (1.25 MB/s), each device spends 50/1.25 = 40 seconds per upload. With gradient compression (10-100x), this drops to 5-50 GB/day. Techniques: top-k sparsification, quantized gradients, or sending only significant parameter deltas.
details.napkin_math: Daily upload: 50 MB x 1,000 x 10 = 500 GB. Per device: 500 MB/day upload. With 10x compression: 50 GB total, 50 MB per device. Upload time at 10 Mbps: 4s per round compressed vs 40s uncompressed.

title: The ANE-GPU Pipeline Split
scenario: You are optimizing a vision-language model on an A17 Pro. The vision encoder runs in 5 ms on the ANE, and the language decoder runs in 8 ms on the GPU. Currently these run sequentially (13 ms total). You propose pipelining: while the GPU decodes frame N, the ANE encodes frame N+1. What is the steady-state latency per frame, and what is the speedup?

details.common_mistake: Claiming the pipelined latency is 5+8=13 ms or even 5 ms. In a pipeline, throughput is limited by the slower stage, but per-frame latency is still the sum for a single frame. The improvement is in throughput (frames per second), not single-frame latency.
details.realistic_solution: In steady-state pipelining, a new result emerges every max(5, 8) = 8 ms. Throughput: 1/8ms = 125 fps vs sequential 1/13ms = 77 fps. Speedup: 13/8 = 1.625x throughput improvement. Note: first-frame latency is still 13 ms, but sustained throughput improves by 62.5%. The ANE is idle 3 ms per cycle, which could be used for preprocessing.
details.napkin_math: Sequential: 13 ms/frame = 77 fps. Pipelined: 8 ms/frame = 125 fps. Speedup: 1.625x. ANE utilization: 5/8 = 62.5%. GPU utilization: 8/8 = 100%.

title: The Privacy Budget Drain
scenario: A mobile health app uses differential privacy with epsilon=1 per query. The analytics team wants to run 100 on-device queries per day against a user's data. Using basic composition, the daily privacy budget would be epsilon=100, which provides almost no privacy. How does advanced composition improve this, and what is the practical implication for query budgeting?

details.common_mistake: Using naive (linear) composition and concluding that differential privacy is useless for frequent queries. Advanced composition via the moments accountant or Renyi DP gives a much tighter bound, making practical deployments feasible.
details.realistic_solution: Naive composition: total epsilon = 100 x 1 = 100 (no meaningful privacy). Advanced composition (square-root law): total epsilon scales as roughly sqrt(100) x 1 = 10. With Renyi DP accounting, even tighter bounds are achievable. Practical implication: use the moments accountant, batch correlated queries, and set a daily privacy budget (e.g., epsilon=5) that limits total queries.
details.napkin_math: Naive: 100 queries x eps=1 = eps_total=100. Advanced (sqrt): sqrt(100) x 1 = eps_total~10. With Renyi DP: potentially eps_total~7-8. To stay under eps_total=5, limit to ~25 queries/day.

title: The On-Device Training Memory Crisis
scenario: You are implementing on-device fine-tuning of a 32-layer transformer on a phone with 8 GB memory. With batch size 4, sequence length 512, and hidden dimension 4096 in FP16, the activation memory for backpropagation across all layers is ~537 MB. The model weights (INT4) are 3.5 GB. Together with KV cache and OS, memory is critically tight. How does gradient checkpointing help, and what does it cost?

details.common_mistake: Assuming gradient checkpointing is free. It trades memory for compute by recomputing activations during the backward pass, typically adding 30-40% wall-clock time. On a battery-powered device, this also means 30-40% more energy per training step.
details.realistic_solution: Without checkpointing: 537 MB activations across 32 layers. With gradient checkpointing (store every sqrt(32)=5-6 layers): ~84 MB activations, saving ~450 MB. The cost: ~33% more compute (recomputing activations for non-checkpointed layers during backward). On a 5W device, this means ~1.65W extra per training step, but the 450 MB savings is critical to fitting in memory.
details.napkin_math: Full activations: 32 layers x 4 x 512 x 4096 x 2B = 537 MB. Checkpointed (5 layers): 5 x 17 MB = 84 MB. Savings: 453 MB. Compute cost: ~33% more FLOPs for backward pass.

title: The Split Inference Latency Trap
scenario: A team proposes split inference: run the first 20 layers of a 32-layer model on-device (5 ms per layer) and the remaining 12 layers on a cloud server (0.5 ms per layer). The network round-trip is 50 ms. Compare total latency for split inference versus running all 32 layers on-device. When does split inference actually help?

details.common_mistake: Assuming split inference is always faster because the cloud is fast. The 50 ms network RTT often dominates, making split inference slower than running everything locally despite the cloud's raw speed advantage.
details.realistic_solution: Split: 20 x 5 ms + 50 ms RTT + 12 x 0.5 ms = 100 + 50 + 6 = 156 ms. All on-device: 32 x 5 ms = 160 ms. Split is only 4 ms faster, and any network jitter erases the gain. Split inference helps when: (1) the model is too large to fit in device memory, or (2) the cloud portion is very large relative to RTT, or (3) you need cloud-only capabilities (e.g., retrieval augmentation).
details.napkin_math: Split: 100ms (device) + 50ms (RTT) + 6ms (cloud) = 156ms. Local: 160ms. Breakeven RTT: 160 - 100 - 6 = 54ms. Any RTT > 54ms makes split slower.

title: The Mobile MoE Memory Illusion
scenario: A research team proposes deploying a Mixture of Experts model on mobile: 8 experts of 500M parameters each (4B total) in INT4, with top-2 routing so only 1B parameters are active per token. They claim it fits in 8 GB because only 0.5 GB of active experts need to be in memory. Explain why this reasoning is flawed for mobile deployment.

details.common_mistake: Confusing active compute parameters with memory footprint. On a server with fast NVMe, you can page experts in and out. On a mobile device, NAND flash is 1000x slower than DRAM, so all experts must reside in memory to avoid catastrophic latency spikes.
details.realistic_solution: Total model memory: 4B x 0.5 bytes (INT4) = 2.0 GB for all experts. Even though only 0.5 GB is active per token, the routing decision changes per token, so all experts must be resident in DRAM. You cannot page from flash (200-500 MB/s) fast enough; loading a cold expert from NAND would add 1-2 seconds of latency per token. The 2 GB footprint fits in 8 GB, but the team's 0.5 GB claim is misleading.
details.napkin_math: All experts: 8 x 500M x 0.5B = 2.0 GB (must be in DRAM). Active per token: 2 x 500M x 0.5B = 0.5 GB. Flash paging penalty: 0.5 GB / 500 MB/s = 1 second per cold expert load. Unacceptable for real-time generation.

title: The On-Device RLHF Memory Wall
scenario: You are implementing on-device RLHF for a mobile assistant. The policy model is 3B params (INT4 = 1.5 GB) and you need a 1B parameter reward model (INT8 = 1 GB) to score responses. Both must be in memory simultaneously during training. On a device with 8 GB unified memory, is this feasible? What are the memory stacks involved?

details.common_mistake: Only counting model weights and forgetting the optimizer states, gradients, and activations required for the RL training loop. The reward model also needs inference activations, even though it is not being trained.
details.realistic_solution: Policy weights: 1.5 GB. Reward model: 1.0 GB. Policy optimizer (AdamW states for LoRA, ~50M trainable params): 50M x 8 bytes = 400 MB. Policy activations + gradients: ~300 MB (with checkpointing). Reward inference activations: ~100 MB. OS + framework overhead: ~1.5 GB. Total: ~4.8 GB. Fits in 8 GB, but barely. Must use LoRA (not full fine-tuning) and gradient checkpointing.
details.napkin_math: Policy (INT4): 1.5 GB. Reward (INT8): 1.0 GB. LoRA optimizer: 0.4 GB. Activations: 0.4 GB. OS: 1.5 GB. Total: 4.8 GB / 8 GB = 60% utilization. Full fine-tuning would add 3B x 8B = 24 GB optimizer states. Impossible.

title: The Replay Buffer Memory Budget
scenario: You are designing a continuous learning system for a mobile recommendation model. The system maintains a replay buffer of 10,000 past interactions stored as 768-dimensional FP16 embeddings plus 64 bytes of metadata per sample. Calculate the buffer size and design a memory-aware eviction policy that prevents unbounded growth on a memory-constrained device.

details.common_mistake: Storing raw input data (images, text) in the replay buffer instead of compact embeddings. A single 224x224 image in FP32 is 600 KB, while its 768-dim FP16 embedding is only 1.5 KB, a 400x compression that makes replay buffers feasible on mobile.
details.realistic_solution: Buffer size: 10,000 x (768 x 2 + 64) = 10,000 x 1,600 bytes = 16 MB. Eviction policy: (1) Hard cap at 16 MB / 10K samples, (2) reservoir sampling to maintain distribution representativeness, (3) priority eviction based on prediction confidence (remove easy examples first), (4) class-balanced eviction to prevent catastrophic forgetting of rare categories.
details.napkin_math: Per sample: 768 dims x 2 bytes + 64 bytes metadata = 1,600 bytes. Buffer: 10K x 1.6 KB = 16 MB. Compare: 10K raw images at 600 KB = 6 GB. Embedding replay is 375x more memory-efficient.

title: The Million-Device Adapter Sync Storm
scenario: Your mobile app personalizes a model using 10 MB LoRA adapters synced from the cloud every 5 minutes. The app has 1 million active users. Calculate the server-side egress bandwidth required and propose a scalable distribution architecture.

details.common_mistake: Only considering per-device bandwidth (10 MB / 300s = 33 KB/s, trivial). The aggregate server-side cost is the real engineering challenge: 1M concurrent downloads create a massive egress bill and bandwidth requirement.
details.realistic_solution: Server egress: 10 MB x 1M devices / 300s = 33 GB/s sustained. At cloud egress pricing ($0.08/GB), this costs 10 MB x 1M x 288 syncs/day = 2,880 TB/day = $230,400/day. Solutions: (1) CDN edge caching for shared adapter updates, (2) push-based delta updates (only changed bytes, ~500 KB), (3) staggered sync windows (not all at once), (4) peer-to-peer gossip for nearby devices.
details.napkin_math: Aggregate BW: 10 MB x 1M / 300s = 33 GB/s. Daily egress: 10 MB x 1M x 288 = 2,880 TB. Cost at $0.08/GB: $230K/day. With delta compression (20x): 1.65 GB/s, $11.5K/day.

title: The Federated Gradient Compression Trade-Off
scenario: In your federated learning system, 100 mobile devices each produce a 50 MB gradient update per round. The team applies 10x gradient compression (top-k sparsification + quantization). Calculate the per-round upload volume and analyze the convergence trade-off of aggressive compression.

details.common_mistake: Assuming compression is lossless. Top-k sparsification discards 90% of gradient components, which can slow convergence by 2-5x in rounds needed, partially offsetting the communication savings. The net benefit depends on whether communication or computation is the bottleneck.
details.realistic_solution: Uncompressed: 100 x 50 MB = 5 GB per round. With 10x compression: 100 x 5 MB = 500 MB per round. At 10 Mbps upload, each device takes 4 seconds instead of 40 seconds. Trade-off: convergence may require 2-3x more rounds (200-300 vs 100), but total communication drops from 500 GB to 100-150 GB across the full training run. Net win if upload bandwidth is the bottleneck.
details.napkin_math: Uncompressed: 100 devices x 50 MB = 5 GB/round. Compressed (10x): 500 MB/round. Upload time: 5 MB / 1.25 MB/s = 4s vs 40s. If convergence needs 2.5x rounds: total = 500 MB x 250 = 125 GB vs 5 GB x 100 = 500 GB. 4x total savings.

title: The Model Distillation Sync Budget
scenario: You run on-device knowledge distillation: a 500 MB teacher model in the cloud periodically pushes updates to guide a 50 MB on-device student. The user has a 100 MB daily cellular data budget. Design a sync protocol that keeps the student model fresh without exceeding the data budget.

details.common_mistake: Sending the full 500 MB teacher model to the device. The student does not need the full teacher; it needs the teacher's soft labels or a compressed delta of the teacher's behavior on the student's local data distribution.
details.realistic_solution: Strategy: (1) Delta compression of teacher weights: ~5% of full model = 25 MB, fits in 100 MB budget. (2) Alternatively, send soft-label distillation targets: for 10K local samples x 32K vocab x FP16, this is too large. Better: send only top-32 logits per sample = 10K x 32 x 4 bytes = 1.3 MB. (3) Hybrid: monthly full sync over WiFi (500 MB), daily delta sync over cellular (25 MB). Budget: 25 MB/day << 100 MB limit.
details.napkin_math: Full teacher: 500 MB (exceeds budget). Delta: 500 MB x 5% = 25 MB (fits). Soft labels (top-32): 10K x 32 x 4B = 1.3 MB (fits easily). Monthly WiFi + daily delta: 25 MB/day = 750 MB/month cellular. Soft labels: 1.3 MB/day = 39 MB/month.

title: The Silent Model Corruption Problem
scenario: After an OS update, users report that your on-device LLM produces nonsensical outputs. Investigation reveals that 3 of the 4,000 one-megabyte weight file chunks were silently corrupted during the filesystem migration. Design a verification system that detects such corruption before inference begins.

details.common_mistake: Only checking a single whole-file hash, which catches corruption but requires re-downloading the entire 4 GB model. Chunk-level checksums allow surgical repair of only the corrupted chunks.
details.realistic_solution: Chunk-level integrity: store a SHA-256 hash (32 bytes) for each 1 MB chunk. Total overhead: 4,000 x 32 bytes = 128 KB (negligible). On startup, verify all chunks in parallel using the ANE or GPU idle cycles (~2 seconds for 4 GB at 2 GB/s read speed). If 3 chunks fail, re-download only 3 MB instead of 4 GB. Additionally, store a Merkle tree root hash for tamper detection.
details.napkin_math: Checksum storage: 4,000 chunks x 32 bytes = 128 KB (0.003% overhead). Verification time: 4 GB / 2 GB/s read = 2s. Repair: 3 MB re-download vs 4 GB full re-download = 1,333x bandwidth savings.

title: The Thermal Throttling Adaptation Loop
scenario: Your mobile AR app runs a real-time segmentation model on the A17 Pro ANE at 35 TOPS. After 10 minutes of sustained use, the thermal management system throttles power from 5W to 3W. The ANE throughput drops proportionally. Design an adaptive inference strategy that maintains acceptable user experience under thermal constraints.

details.common_mistake: Ignoring thermal throttling entirely and having the app stutter or crash when throughput drops. Another mistake is statically reducing quality at app launch, wasting the initial thermal headroom.
details.realistic_solution: Throttled throughput: 35 x (3/5) = 21 TOPS (60% of peak). Adaptive strategy: (1) Monitor thermal state via OS APIs, (2) maintain 3 model tiers: full (35 TOPS, 30fps), medium (21 TOPS, 20fps with smaller input resolution), lite (14 TOPS, 15fps with aggressive pruning), (3) smooth transitions over 2-3 seconds to avoid visual jarring, (4) predictive throttling: start reducing quality 30s before expected thermal limit based on temperature trajectory.
details.napkin_math: Peak: 35 TOPS at 5W. Throttled: 35 x 3/5 = 21 TOPS at 3W (60% throughput). Frame budget: full model needs 30 TOPS for 30fps. At 21 TOPS, max sustainable is ~21 fps. Switching to medium model (15 TOPS for 30fps) restores 30fps under throttling.
